Ultra-sensitive determination of carcinogenic
aﬂatoxins in food matrices using a novel magnetic
bimetallic ZIF-8@chitosan sorbent and dispersive

Alireza Shams,
and Mahdi Ghorbani

*b

*a Foroughalzaman Kazempoor Mofrad

a

A dispersive micro-solid phase extraction (D-m-SPE) method coupled with HPLC-FLD was developed for
the sensitive determination of carcinogenic aﬂatoxins. A novel magnetic composite sorbent was
synthesized by integrating bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-8, chitosan, and Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Statistical
comparison revealed that bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-8 exhibited signiﬁcantly enhanced aﬂatoxin extraction
eﬃciency over its monometallic counterpart, with subsequent functionalization using chitosan and
magnetization further improving performance. Key extraction parameters were systematically optimized
using experimental design. The validated method demonstrated wide linearity (0.02–120 ng mL−1; R2 >
0.993), and low detection limits (LODs: 0.006–0.07 ng mL−1), and quantiﬁcation limits (LOQs: 0.02–0.21
ng mL−1). Precision ranged from 3.7–4.7% (intra-day) to 4.5–6.2% (inter-day). Given the critical need to

Introduction

Aatoxins, which are highly carcinogenic secondary metabolites
predominantly biosynthesized by the fungal species Aspergillus
avus and Aspergillus parasiticus, pose a pervasive global threat
to food safety, public health, and agricultural economies, thus
demanding rigorous scientic investigation.1,2 Contamination
of critical staple crops, including maize, groundnuts, tree nuts,
spices, and oilseeds, occurs both pre- and post-harvest, partic-
ularly under warm, humid conditions that are increasingly
prevalent as a result of climate change.3,4 This widespread
contamination leads to signicant human exposure through
the food chain.5 The human health consequences are severe
and multifaceted: chronic ingestion of low levels, particularly
aatoxin B1, is a well-established primary risk factor for hepa-
tocellular carcinoma, while acute exposure can induce fatal
aatoxicosis characterized by hemorrhagic necrosis of the
liver.6 Beyond their potent carcinogenicity, aatoxins exhibit
signicant immunosuppressive, mutagenic, and teratogenic
eﬀects,
to childhood growth impairment,

assessment, eﬀective regulatory enforcement, and the devel-
opment of targeted mitigation strategies. High-Performance
Liquid Chromatography (HPLC), frequently coupled with uo-

the benchmark analytical technique for this purpose, oﬀering
the requisite high sensitivity for detection at stringent regula-

bodies (e.g., EU, FDA, Codex Alimentarius) and exceptional

individual aatoxins (B1, B2, G1, G2, M1) within complex food
and feed matrices.10,11

However, the inherent complexity of these matrices presents
a signicant analytical challenge, necessitating eﬃcient and
reliable sample preparation prior to HPLC analysis to ensure
accuracy and sensitivity.12,13 Co-extracted compounds such as

Among available techniques, dispersive micro solid-phase
extraction (D-m-SPE) has emerged as a highly advantageous
approach for aatoxin analysis.16 D-m-SPE involves the direct
dispersion of a small quantity of solid sorbent material into the
sample extract, facilitating rapid and eﬃcient adsorption of
target analytes.17 This method oﬀers substantial benets over
conventional solid-phase extraction (SPE), including drastically
reduced solvent consumption (aligning with green analytical
chemistry principles), shorter processing times, simplied
procedures that minimizing manual handling, and lower
operational costs.16,18 Crucially, the versatility of D-m-SPE allows
for strategic selection of the sorbent material to optimize
selectivity and enrichment factors for aatoxins, even at ultra-

performance characteristics of the sorbent material, which acts
as the active interface for the selective capture and pre-
concentration of
simultaneously
excluding matrix interferents.16,21 Key parameters such as
sorption capacity, selectivity, sorption/desorption kinetics, and
reusability are dictated by the sorbent's chemical composition,
surface area, pore structure, and surface functionality.22,23 In
this context, Metal–Organic Frameworks (MOFs) – crystalline
porous materials constructed from metal
ions or clusters
coordinated to multidentate organic linkers – represent a revo-
lutionary class of sorbents for D-m-SPE.24 Their exceptional
suitability arises from their ultra-high surface areas and
analyte pre-concentration,
porosity,

functionality to enhance aﬃnity and selectivity for specic

hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic eﬀects).25,26 Bimetallic MOFs,
incorporating two distinct metal ions within a single frame-
work, oﬀer
enhanced performance as D-m-SPE
sorbents.27,28 The synergistic interaction between the dual
metals modulates properties such as Lewis acidity, redox

engineering of active sites optimized for strong interaction with

matrix tolerance. Furthermore, functionalization of bimetallic

rapid and convenient magnetic separation aer extraction,
signicantly streamlining the D-m-SPE workow.31,32

2.3. Sorbent preparation

2.3.1. Bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-8. A methanolic solution (10
mL) containing zinc nitrate hexahydrate (1.875 mmol) and
cobalt(II) nitrate hexahydrate (0.469 mmol) was prepared.
Separately, 2-methylimidazole (7.5 mmol) was dissolved in
methanol (15 mL). Both solutions were sonicated for 5 minutes
to ensure complete dissolution. Subsequently, the metal salt
solution was added incrementally to the ligand solution under
continuous sonication: 5 mL was added over 3 minutes, fol-
lowed by an additional 5 mL added over 10 minutes. The
resulting precipitate was isolated by centrifugation, washed
thoroughly with methanol, and dried under vacuum.

2.3.2. Magnetic bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-8@chitosan. Chito-
san (0.5 g) was dissolved in 25 mL of aqueous acetic acid (20%
w/v) with magnetic stirring for a minimum of 10 minutes.
Bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-8 (0.4 g) and magnetite (Fe3O4, 0.4 g) were

2.4. Sorbent characterization

Fig. 1 presents the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image
of (a) the bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-8 and (b) the magnetic bimetallic
Co/Zn ZIF-8@chitosan nanocomposite, acquired at magnica-
tions of 50.0 kx and 70.0 kx, respectively. The pristine bimetallic
Co/Zn ZIF-8 (Fig. 1a) exhibits a well-dened rhombic dodeca-
hedral morphology characteristic of ZIF-8 frameworks, with
sharp edges and smooth facets averaging 500–600 nm in
diameter, indicative of high crystallinity and phase purity. In
the magnetic Co/Zn ZIF-8@chitosan composite
contrast,
(Fig. 1b) reveals a distinct core–shell architecture, where Fe3O4
nanoparticles (5–20 nm) are uniformly embedded within
a porous chitosan matrix that conformally encapsulates the
bimetallic ZIF-8 crystals. This hierarchical structure results in
a roughened surface topography with increased nano- and
mesoporosity (pore diameter ∼2–50 nm), attributed to chito-
san's brillar network cross-linking the magnetic and ZIF
components. The morphological evolution from crystalline
polyhedra (Fig. 1a)
to an interconnected porous hydrogel
composite (Fig. 1b) substantially enhances aatoxin adsorption
eﬃcacy by: (i) augmenting accessible surface area (as conrmed
by BET analysis, vide infra), (ii) introducing multifunctional

binding sites (–NH2, –OH) from chitosan for hydrogen bonding
and electrostatic interactions with aatoxins, and (iii) enabling
magnetic separation (via embedded Fe3O4) for operational
convenience.

The EDX patterns of the bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-8 and the
magnetic bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-8@chitosan composite are

Iron (Fe) appears, providing direct evidence for the incorpora-

the substantial oxygen content contributed by both the Fe3O4
nanoparticles (Fe3O4) and the abundant hydroxyl (–OH) and
ether (–C–O–C–) groups within the chitosan polysaccharide

2.5. Extraction procedure
A 15.0 mL aliquot of sample solution containing target aa-
toxins (AFB1, AFB2, AFG1, AFG2) was transferred to a glass vial.
Sodium chloride (0.375 g, 2.5% w/v) was added to enhance ionic
strength, followed by pH adjustment to 7.4 using 1.0 mL of
0.1 M phosphate buﬀer (prepared from Na2HPO4/NaH2PO4).
Magnetic sorbent (40.0 mg) was dispersed into the solution and
agitated at 500 rpm for 8 min to facilitate adsorption. The
sorbent was magnetically separated (NdFeB magnet, 1.4 T eld
strength; 2 min contact) and the supernatant decanted. For
desorption, 120 mL HPLC-grade acetonitrile was introduced and
vortex-mixed (500 rpm, 12.5 min). Aer magnetic separation (2
min), 20.0 mL of the acetonitrile phase was collected via
microsyringe and injected into the HPLC-FLD system for
quantitative analysis of aatoxins.

2.6. Real sample preparation

Bottled commercial mineral waters from three companies,
(Vata Brand, Pak Ab Sabalan Mineral Water Co, Ardabil, Iran),
(Damavand Mineral Waters Co, Tehran, Iran), and (Miva
Mineral Water Co, Amol, Iran), was purchased from a local
supermarket (Mashhad, Iran). Agricultural samples included
rice samples (Oryza sativa L. grains) of the traditional Hashemi
cultivar and commercial Fajr brand obtained from retail
suppliers in Mashhad, along with commercially available raw
peanut kernels (Arachis hypogaea L.) procured from local
supermarkets in the same metropolitan area. All samples were
immediately transported to the laboratory under dark condi-
tions at 4 °C, until analysis to preserve analyte integrity and
prevent aatoxin degradation.

Representative batches of peanut kernels or rice grains (10.0
g) are ground to achieve a homogeneous powder (particle size
#0.5 mm), sieved through a 20-mesh screen (0.85 mm aper-
ture), and dried at 60 °C for 24 h to standardize moisture
content (<10% w/w). Aatoxins are known to be thermally stable
compounds, with studies conrming their stability during
conventional drying processes at temperatures below 100 °C.33,34
Aliquots (0.2 ± 0.01 g) of dried matrix are extracted with acidi-
ed solvent (5.0 mL of acetonitrile: water: formic acid, 70 : 29 : 1
v/v/v) via vigorous shaking (250 rpm, 60 min). The mixture is
centrifuged (5000×g, 10 min), and the supernatant is ltered
through Whatman No. 4 lter paper. The ltrate is evaporated
to dryness under N2 at 40 °C. The residue is reconstituted in
15.0 mL of deionized water and vortex-mixed (1 min) to yield
a matrix-matched solution. For recovery studies, this solution is

spiked with aatoxin standards (AFB1/AFB2/AFG1/AFG2 in
acetonitrile) to achieve nal concentrations of 5.0 or 20.0 ng
mL

−1, followed by d-mSPE and HPLC-FLD analysis.
To prepare the bottled commercial mineral water samples,
50 mL of the water sample was selected and centrifuged at
5000 rpm for 10 minutes to remove suspended particulates. The
supernatant is ltered through a 0.45 mm nylon membrane lter
to eliminate residual colloids prior to dispersive micro solid-
phase extraction (d-mSPE) and HPLC-FLD analysis. Then, the
samples were spiked with a standard solution of Aatoxin to
−1. The
achieve nominal concentrations of 5.0 or 20.0 ng mL
spiked samples were reanalyzed to determine Aatoxin
concentration and calculate the recovery.

3 Results and discussions
3.1. The optimization of the procedure

8@chitosan. Initial extraction eﬃciencies (ER% ± standard
deviation) revealed distinct performance diﬀerences (Table S1).

experimental conditions, and the resulting data were used for
both quantitative evaluation of sorbent performance and
subsequent statistical analysis. To statistically validate these

2.5. Extraction procedure
A 15.0 mL aliquot of sample solution containing target aa-
toxins (AFB1, AFB2, AFG1, AFG2) was transferred to a glass vial.
Sodium chloride (0.375 g, 2.5% w/v) was added to enhance ionic
strength, followed by pH adjustment to 7.4 using 1.0 mL of
0.1 M phosphate buﬀer (prepared from Na2HPO4/NaH2PO4).
Magnetic sorbent (40.0 mg) was dispersed into the solution and
agitated at 500 rpm for 8 min to facilitate adsorption. The
sorbent was magnetically separated (NdFeB magnet, 1.4 T eld
strength; 2 min contact) and the supernatant decanted. For
desorption, 120 mL HPLC-grade acetonitrile was introduced and
vortex-mixed (500 rpm, 12.5 min). Aer magnetic separation (2
min), 20.0 mL of the acetonitrile phase was collected via
microsyringe and injected into the HPLC-FLD system for
quantitative analysis of aatoxins.

2.6. Real sample preparation

Bottled commercial mineral waters from three companies,
(Vata Brand, Pak Ab Sabalan Mineral Water Co, Ardabil, Iran),
(Damavand Mineral Waters Co, Tehran, Iran), and (Miva
Mineral Water Co, Amol, Iran), was purchased from a local
supermarket (Mashhad, Iran). Agricultural samples included
rice samples (Oryza sativa L. grains) of the traditional Hashemi
cultivar and commercial Fajr brand obtained from retail
suppliers in Mashhad, along with commercially available raw
peanut kernels (Arachis hypogaea L.) procured from local
supermarkets in the same metropolitan area. All samples were
immediately transported to the laboratory under dark condi-
tions at 4 °C, until analysis to preserve analyte integrity and
prevent aatoxin degradation.

Representative batches of peanut kernels or rice grains (10.0
g) are ground to achieve a homogeneous powder (particle size
#0.5 mm), sieved through a 20-mesh screen (0.85 mm aper-
ture), and dried at 60 °C for 24 h to standardize moisture
content (<10% w/w). Aatoxins are known to be thermally stable
compounds, with studies conrming their stability during
conventional drying processes at temperatures below 100 °C.33,34
Aliquots (0.2 ± 0.01 g) of dried matrix are extracted with acidi-
ed solvent (5.0 mL of acetonitrile: water: formic acid, 70 : 29 : 1
v/v/v) via vigorous shaking (250 rpm, 60 min). The mixture is
centrifuged (5000×g, 10 min), and the supernatant is ltered
through Whatman No. 4 lter paper. The ltrate is evaporated
to dryness under N2 at 40 °C. The residue is reconstituted in
15.0 mL of deionized water and vortex-mixed (1 min) to yield
a matrix-matched solution. For recovery studies, this solution is

spiked with aatoxin standards (AFB1/AFB2/AFG1/AFG2 in
acetonitrile) to achieve nal concentrations of 5.0 or 20.0 ng
mL

−1, followed by d-mSPE and HPLC-FLD analysis.

3 Results and discussions
3.1. The optimization of the procedure

3.1.1. Type of sorbent. The optimization of sorbents for
aatoxin extraction was evaluated using four sorbents: Fe3O4,
Zn ZIF-8, bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-8, and bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-
8@chitosan. Initial extraction eﬃciencies (ER% ± standard
deviation) revealed distinct performance diﬀerences (Table S1).
All experiments were conducted in triplicate under identical
experimental conditions, and the resulting data were used for
both quantitative evaluation of sorbent performance and
subsequent statistical analysis. To statistically validate these
results, normality testing was rst conducted via the Shapiro–
Wilk test. All sorbents exhibited non-signicant deviations from
normality p-value > 0.05), conrming parametric test assump-
tions (Table S2). Subsequent one-way ANOVA demonstrated
highly signicant diﬀerences among the sorbents (F(3, 8) =
80.968, p-value < 0.001), with between-group variation (sum of
squares = 2543.570) signicantly exceeding within-group vari-
ation (sum of squares = 83.772) (Table S3). Post-hoc Tukey HSD
tests further elucidated inter-sorbent diﬀerences (Table S4).
Bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-8@chitosan outperformed all others (p-
value < 0.001), showing mean eﬃciency gains of 41.02% over
Fe3O4, 20.44% over Zn ZIF-8, and 17.50% over bimetallic Co/Zn
ZIF-8. The bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-8 and Zn ZIF-8 both signi-
cantly surpassed Fe3O4 (p-value < 0.001), but no signicant
diﬀerence existed between them (p-value = 0.693). Similarly,
while bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-8@chitosan was superior to its non-
chitosan counterpart (p-value = 0.001), the unmodied bime-
tallic Co/Zn ZIF-8 did not statistically exceed Zn ZIF-8 (p-value =
0.693). These results demonstrate that bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-
8@chitosan is the optimal sorbent for aatoxin extraction,
leveraging chitosan functionalization to signicantly enhance
eﬃciency. To provide a quantitative understanding of the
adsorption behavior, the extraction eﬃciency data (Table S1)
were correlated with the physicochemical properties of the

The observed diﬀerences in aatoxin extraction eﬃciency
among the sorbents can be attributed to their distinct structural
and functional properties, which directly inuence their aﬃnity
for aatoxins. Fe3O4 nanoparticles exhibit the lowest extraction
eﬃciency (38.45% ± 1.80), primarily due to their lack of
intrinsic porosity and limited functional groups for specic
interactions with aatoxins. In contrast, Zn ZIF-8 and bimetallic
Co/Zn ZIF-8 leverage the inherent advantages of MOFs,
including high surface area, tunable pore sizes, and hydro-
phobic cavities that facilitate p–p stacking interactions with the
aromatic ring of aatoxins. Beyond the statistical validation, the
mechanistic basis for the enhanced performance of bimetallic
Co/Zn ZIF-8 warrants closer examination. The partial substitu-
tion of Zn2+ with Co2+ within the ZIF-8 framework introduces
two complementary eﬀects that collectively improve aatoxin
aﬃnity. First, Co2+ possesses higher Lewis acidity compared to
Zn2+ due to its electronic conguration and unpaired d-
electrons. This enhanced Lewis acidity strengthens acid–base
interactions with the electron-rich carbonyl and lactone groups
present in aatoxin structures (e.g., the b-dicarbonyl system of
aatoxin B1). Specically, Co2+ sites can act as stronger electron
pair acceptors, facilitating coordination-like interactions with
the oxygen atoms of aatoxin's lactone ring and furan moie-
ties—functional groups critical
to aatoxin toxicity and
planarity. Second, the incorporation of Co2+ subtly modies the
pore environment of ZIF-8. Although the overall SOD topology is
preserved, the slightly diﬀerent ionic radius and coordination

Chitosan, a linear polysaccharide composed of randomly

zation. TPP, a polyanion, cross-links chitosan chains through
ionic gelation–electrostatic interactions between TPP's phos-
phate groups and chitosan's protonated amino groups during
synthesis (typically conducted under acidic conditions). Once
formed, this cross-linked structure remains stable at pH 7.4 and
fundamentally transforms the chitosan architecture:
it

cross-linked network prevents dense chain packing and main-

additional phosphate groups that may participate in hydrogen
bonding with aatoxins at pH 7.4, further enriching the binding
landscape.

3.1.2. Type of desorption solvent. The optimization of
desorption solvents for aatoxin extraction was evaluated using
ve solvents: methanol, ethanol, 2-propanol, acetonitrile, and
acetone. Extraction eﬃciencies (ER% ± standard deviation)
revealed distinct performance variations (Table S5). Normality
testing via the Shapiro–Wilk test conrmed all datasets adhered
to a normal distribution (p-value > 0.05), satisfying parametric
test assumptions (Table S6). One-way ANOVA demonstrated
signicant diﬀerences among solvents (F(4, 10) = 13.970, p-
value < 0.001), with between-group variation (sum of squares =
495.188) substantially exceeding within-group variation (sum of
squares = 88.614) (Table S7). Post-hoc Tukey HSD analysis
delineated solvent-specic performance (Table S8). Acetonitrile
exhibited signicantly higher eﬃciency than ethanol (p-value =
0.027), 2-propanol (p-value = 0.024), and acetone (p-value <
0.001), though its advantage over methanol was marginally non-
signicant (p-value = 0.052). Methanol, ethanol, and 2-propanol
showed no statistical diﬀerences (p-value > 0.986), forming
a homogeneous subgroup. Acetone underperformed signi-
cantly versus all solvents (p-value < 0.027) except 2-propanol (p-
value = 0.027), with the largest eﬃciency decit observed
against acetonitrile (18.11%, p-value < 0.001). These results
highlight acetonitrile as the optimal desorption solvent,
attributable to its intermediate polarity (dielectric constant 3 =
37.5, 20 °C), aprotic nature, and strong dipole moment (3.2 D),
which enhance solvation of moderately polar aatoxins through
dipole–dipole and van der Waals interactions. Methanol's
comparable performance (p-value = 0.052) reects its similar
polarity (3 = 32.6, 20 °C) and protic character, enabling
hydrogen bonding with aatoxin lactone groups. Acetone's
inferiority stems from its lower polarity (3 = 20.6, 20 °C) and
potential ketone-aldehyde reactivity with aatoxins, reducing
extraction eﬃcacy.35

3.1.3. Screening step. Extraction eﬃciency in D-m-SPE
depends critically on multiple interacting factors, including pH,
Implementing

a systematic experimental design is essential to screen these
factors eﬃciently while minimizing resource-intensive trial-
and-error approaches. The Denitive Screening Design (DSD)
is particularly advantageous for this purpose, enabling robust
evaluation of k factors with only 2 k + 1 experimental runs.36,37
This design simultaneously identies dominant main eﬀects,
detects curvature, and reveals potential factor interactions,
thereby accelerating optimization of the D-m-SPE process.38,39
The approach enhances methodological robustness, reduces
experimental costs, and expedites the development of reliable
aatoxin analysis protocols.

Seven continuous factors were investigated: sample solution
volume (A), pH (B), sorbent mass (C), extraction time (D),
desorption solvent volume (E), desorption time (F), and NaCl
concentration (G). The DSD matrix (Table S9) details coded
factor levels (−1, 0, +1) corresponding to experimentally dened
minima, center points, and maxima. Analysis of variance
(ANOVA) at 95% condence (a = 0.05) was applied to extraction
recovery (ER%) data (Table S10), with p-values < 0.05 indicating
statistical signicance. ER% values represent the mean of
triplicate measurements performed under identical experi-
mental conditions.

The ANOVA results demonstrate a statistically signicant
overall model (p-value = 0.0077), conrming that the factors
collectively explain substantial variability in ER% (Table S10).
Three factors signicantly inuenced aatoxin extraction: pH
(B) exhibited the strongest eﬀect (p-value = 0.0042), under-
scoring its critical role in extraction eﬃciency. Extraction time
(D) (p-value = 0.0106) and desorption solvent volume (E) (p-
value = 0.0094) also showed signicant impacts, highlighting
their importance in the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. In
contrast, desorption time (F) approached signicance (p-value
= 0.0556) but exceeded the 95% condence threshold. The
remaining factors—sample volume (A)
(p-value = 0.9193),
sorbent mass (C) (p-value = 0.4081), and NaCl concentration (G)
(p-value = 0.2984)—were statistically insignicant, indicating
minimal inuence on ER% within the tested ranges.

These ndings streamline process optimization by identi-
fying pH, extraction time, and desorption solvent volume as key
manipulable variables. Insignicant factors were xed at their
DSD center points: sample volume at 15.0 mL, sorbent mass at
40.0 mg, desorption time at 12.5 min, and NaCl concentration
at 2.50% (w/v) during subsequent optimization phases.

The ANOVA results for the Central Composite Design (Table
S11) demonstrate a statistically signicant quadratic model (p <
0.0001) for aatoxin extraction using magnetic bimetallic Co/Zn
ZIF-8@chitosan, explaining 98.3% of the variability in extrac-
tion recovery (ER%). The model's high F-value (63.87) and non-
signicant lack of t (p = 0.1105) conrm its robustness for
optimization. All three linear factors exhibit strong signicance:
pH (A) (p < 0.0001, F = 51.06) dominates due to its control over
sorbent-analyte electrostatic interactions; extraction time (B) (p
< 0.0001, F = 98.66) underscores kinetic limitations in adsorp-
tion; and desorption solvent volume (C) (p < 0.0001, F = 90.23)
dictates elution eﬃciency. Signicant quadratic curvature (p #
0.0011 for A2, B2, C2) reveals non-monotonic responses, con-
rming that eﬃciency maxima exist within the experimental
domain. Interaction analysis identies two signicant binary
eﬀects: pH × extraction time (AB) (p = 0.0006) demonstrates
synergy (e.g., alkaline pH extends optimal binding duration),
and extraction time × solvent volume (BC) (p = 0.0125) indi-
cates time-dependent solvent eﬃcacy for desorption. The non-
signicant pH × solvent volume (AC) interaction (p = 0.7798)
implies these factors operate independently. The low residual

error (SS = 76.02) relative to total variation further validates
model adequacy. These results necessitate a balanced optimi-
zation strategy that accounts for the parabolic responses of
individual factors and leverages the synergistic AB and BC
interactions to maximize ER%.

As shown in Table S14, the matrix eﬀect for water samples
was minimal, with % ME values ranging from 96.8% to 97.3%
across all four aatoxins. This negligible suppression (less than

97.6%). Peanut samples exhibited the most pronounced matrix
eﬀect, with % ME values ranging from 86.1% to 87.5%. The
signal suppression (approximately 12–14%) is attributable to
the complex lipid-rich matrix of peanuts, which introduces co-
extracted fatty acids and other hydrophobic compounds
during sample preparation. The slightly higher suppression
observed for AFB2 (86.1%) compared to AFB1 (87.3%) suggests
minor diﬀerences in susceptibility to matrix interference based
on analyte polarity. The varying degrees of matrix eﬀect across
the three matrices highlight the importance of matrix-specic
method validation. The use of matrix-matched calibration
standards for rice and peanut samples eﬀectively compensates
for the observed suppression, ensuring reliable quantication
as conrmed by the satisfactory recovery rates (92.8–94.3% for
peanuts).

Introduction

Aatoxins, which are highly carcinogenic secondary metabolites
predominantly biosynthesized by the fungal species Aspergillus
avus and Aspergillus parasiticus, pose a pervasive global threat
to food safety, public health, and agricultural economies, thus
demanding rigorous scientic investigation.1,2 Contamination
of critical staple crops, including maize, groundnuts, tree nuts,
spices, and oilseeds, occurs both pre- and post-harvest, partic-
ularly under warm, humid conditions that are increasingly
prevalent as a result of climate change.3,4 This widespread
contamination leads to signicant human exposure through
the food chain.5 The human health consequences are severe
and multifaceted: chronic ingestion of low levels, particularly
aatoxin B1, is a well-established primary risk factor for hepa-
tocellular carcinoma, while acute exposure can induce fatal
aatoxicosis characterized by hemorrhagic necrosis of the
liver.6 Beyond their potent carcinogenicity, aatoxins exhibit
signicant immunosuppressive, mutagenic, and teratogenic
eﬀects,
to childhood growth impairment,

individual aatoxins (B1, B2, G1, G2, M1) within complex food
and feed matrices.10,11

target analytes.17 This method oﬀers substantial benets over
conventional solid-phase extraction (SPE), including drastically
reduced solvent consumption (aligning with green analytical
chemistry principles), shorter processing times, simplied
procedures that minimizing manual handling, and lower
operational costs.16,18 Crucially, the versatility of D-m-SPE allows

performance characteristics of the sorbent material, which acts
as the active interface for the selective capture and pre-

sorption capacity, selectivity, sorption/desorption kinetics, and
reusability are dictated by the sorbent's chemical composition,
surface area, pore structure, and surface functionality.22,23 In
this context, Metal–Organic Frameworks (MOFs) – crystalline
porous materials constructed from metal
ions or clusters

incorporating two distinct metal ions within a single frame-

engineering of active sites optimized for strong interaction with

2.5. Extraction procedure
A 15.0 mL aliquot of sample solution containing target aa-
toxins (AFB1, AFB2, AFG1, AFG2) was transferred to a glass vial.
Sodium chloride (0.375 g, 2.5% w/v) was added to enhance ionic
strength, followed by pH adjustment to 7.4 using 1.0 mL of
0.1 M phosphate buﬀer (prepared from Na2HPO4/NaH2PO4).
Magnetic sorbent (40.0 mg) was dispersed into the solution and
agitated at 500 rpm for 8 min to facilitate adsorption. The
sorbent was magnetically separated (NdFeB magnet, 1.4 T eld
strength; 2 min contact) and the supernatant decanted. For
desorption, 120 mL HPLC-grade acetonitrile was introduced and
vortex-mixed (500 rpm, 12.5 min). Aer magnetic separation (2
min), 20.0 mL of the acetonitrile phase was collected via
microsyringe and injected into the HPLC-FLD system for
quantitative analysis of aatoxins.

2.6. Real sample preparation

Bottled commercial mineral waters from three companies,
(Vata Brand, Pak Ab Sabalan Mineral Water Co, Ardabil, Iran),
(Damavand Mineral Waters Co, Tehran, Iran), and (Miva
Mineral Water Co, Amol, Iran), was purchased from a local
supermarket (Mashhad, Iran). Agricultural samples included
rice samples (Oryza sativa L. grains) of the traditional Hashemi
cultivar and commercial Fajr brand obtained from retail
suppliers in Mashhad, along with commercially available raw
peanut kernels (Arachis hypogaea L.) procured from local
supermarkets in the same metropolitan area. All samples were
immediately transported to the laboratory under dark condi-
tions at 4 °C, until analysis to preserve analyte integrity and
prevent aatoxin degradation.

Representative batches of peanut kernels or rice grains (10.0
g) are ground to achieve a homogeneous powder (particle size
#0.5 mm), sieved through a 20-mesh screen (0.85 mm aper-
ture), and dried at 60 °C for 24 h to standardize moisture
content (<10% w/w). Aatoxins are known to be thermally stable
compounds, with studies conrming their stability during
conventional drying processes at temperatures below 100 °C.33,34
Aliquots (0.2 ± 0.01 g) of dried matrix are extracted with acidi-
ed solvent (5.0 mL of acetonitrile: water: formic acid, 70 : 29 : 1
v/v/v) via vigorous shaking (250 rpm, 60 min). The mixture is
centrifuged (5000×g, 10 min), and the supernatant is ltered
through Whatman No. 4 lter paper. The ltrate is evaporated
to dryness under N2 at 40 °C. The residue is reconstituted in
15.0 mL of deionized water and vortex-mixed (1 min) to yield
a matrix-matched solution. For recovery studies, this solution is

spiked with aatoxin standards (AFB1/AFB2/AFG1/AFG2 in
acetonitrile) to achieve nal concentrations of 5.0 or 20.0 ng
mL

−1, followed by d-mSPE and HPLC-FLD analysis.

Introduction

Aatoxins, which are highly carcinogenic secondary metabolites
predominantly biosynthesized by the fungal species Aspergillus
avus and Aspergillus parasiticus, pose a pervasive global threat
to food safety, public health, and agricultural economies, thus
demanding rigorous scientic investigation.1,2 Contamination
of critical staple crops, including maize, groundnuts, tree nuts,
spices, and oilseeds, occurs both pre- and post-harvest, partic-
ularly under warm, humid conditions that are increasingly
prevalent as a result of climate change.3,4 This widespread
contamination leads to signicant human exposure through
the food chain.5 The human health consequences are severe
and multifaceted: chronic ingestion of low levels, particularly
aatoxin B1, is a well-established primary risk factor for hepa-
tocellular carcinoma, while acute exposure can induce fatal
aatoxicosis characterized by hemorrhagic necrosis of the
liver.6 Beyond their potent carcinogenicity, aatoxins exhibit
signicant immunosuppressive, mutagenic, and teratogenic
eﬀects,

2.5. Extraction procedure
A 15.0 mL aliquot of sample solution containing target aa-
toxins (AFB1, AFB2, AFG1, AFG2) was transferred to a glass vial.
Sodium chloride (0.375 g, 2.5% w/v) was added to enhance ionic
strength, followed by pH adjustment to 7.4 using 1.0 mL of
0.1 M phosphate buﬀer (prepared from Na2HPO4/NaH2PO4).
Magnetic sorbent (40.0 mg) was dispersed into the solution and
agitated at 500 rpm for 8 min to facilitate adsorption. The
sorbent was magnetically separated (NdFeB magnet, 1.4 T eld
strength; 2 min contact) and the supernatant decanted. For
desorption, 120 mL HPLC-grade acetonitrile was introduced and
vortex-mixed (500 rpm, 12.5 min). Aer magnetic separation (2
min), 20.0 mL of the acetonitrile phase was collected via
microsyringe and injected into the HPLC-FLD system for
quantitative analysis of aatoxins.

2.6. Real sample preparation

Bottled commercial mineral waters from three companies,
(Vata Brand, Pak Ab Sabalan Mineral Water Co, Ardabil, Iran),
(Damavand Mineral Waters Co, Tehran, Iran), and (Miva
Mineral Water Co, Amol, Iran), was purchased from a local
supermarket (Mashhad, Iran). Agricultural samples included
rice samples (Oryza sativa L. grains) of the traditional Hashemi
cultivar and commercial Fajr brand obtained from retail
suppliers in Mashhad, along with commercially available raw
peanut kernels (Arachis hypogaea L.) procured from local
supermarkets in the same metropolitan area. All samples were
immediately transported to the laboratory under dark condi-
tions at 4 °C, until analysis to preserve analyte integrity and
prevent aatoxin degradation.

Representative batches of peanut kernels or rice grains (10.0
g) are ground to achieve a homogeneous powder (particle size
#0.5 mm), sieved through a 20-mesh screen (0.85 mm aper-
ture), and dried at 60 °C for 24 h to standardize moisture
content (<10% w/w). Aatoxins are known to be thermally stable
compounds, with studies conrming their stability during
conventional drying processes at temperatures below 100 °C.33,34
Aliquots (0.2 ± 0.01 g) of dried matrix are extracted with acidi-
ed solvent (5.0 mL of acetonitrile: water: formic acid, 70 : 29 : 1
v/v/v) via vigorous shaking (250 rpm, 60 min). The mixture is
centrifuged (5000×g, 10 min), and the supernatant is ltered
through Whatman No. 4 lter paper. The ltrate is evaporated
to dryness under N2 at 40 °C. The residue is reconstituted in
15.0 mL of deionized water and vortex-mixed (1 min) to yield
a matrix-matched solution. For recovery studies, this solution is

spiked with aatoxin standards (AFB1/AFB2/AFG1/AFG2 in
acetonitrile) to achieve nal concentrations of 5.0 or 20.0 ng
mL

−1, followed by d-mSPE and HPLC-FLD analysis.
To prepare the bottled commercial mineral water samples,
50 mL of the water sample was selected and centrifuged at
5000 rpm for 10 minutes to remove suspended particulates. The
supernatant is ltered through a 0.45 mm nylon membrane lter
to eliminate residual colloids prior to dispersive micro solid-
phase extraction (d-mSPE) and HPLC-FLD analysis. Then, the
samples were spiked with a standard solution of Aatoxin to
−1. The
achieve nominal concentrations of 5.0 or 20.0 ng mL
spiked samples were reanalyzed to determine Aatoxin
concentration and calculate the recovery.

3.1. The optimization of the procedure

3.1.1. Type of sorbent. The optimization of sorbents for
aatoxin extraction was evaluated using four sorbents: Fe3O4,
Zn ZIF-8, bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-8, and bimetallic Co/Zn ZIF-
8@chitosan. Initial extraction eﬃciencies (ER% ± standard
deviation) revealed distinct performance diﬀerences (Table S1).
All experiments were conducted in triplicate under identical
experimental conditions, and the resulting data were used for
both quantitative evaluation of sorbent performance and
subsequent statistical analysis. To statistically validate these

Among available techniques, dispersive micro solid-phase
extraction (D-m-SPE) has emerged as a highly advantageous
approach for aatoxin analysis.16 D-m-SPE involves the direct
dispersion of a small quantity of solid sorbent material into the
sample extract, facilitating rapid and eﬃcient adsorption of
target analytes.17 This method oﬀers substantial benets over
conventional solid-phase extraction (SPE), including drastically
reduced solvent consumption (aligning with green analytical
chemistry principles), shorter processing times, simplied
procedures that minimizing manual handling, and lower
operational costs.16,18 Crucially, the versatility of D-m-SPE allows
for strategic selection of the sorbent material to optimize
selectivity and enrichment factors for aatoxins, even at ultra-
thereby enhancing subsequent HPLC perfor-

simultaneously
excluding matrix interferents.16,21 Key parameters such as
sorption capacity, selectivity, sorption/desorption kinetics, and
reusability are dictated by the sorbent's chemical composition,
surface area, pore structure, and surface functionality.22,23 In
this context, Metal–Organic Frameworks (MOFs) – crystalline
porous materials constructed from metal
ions or clusters
coordinated to multidentate organic linkers – represent a revo-
lutionary class of sorbents for D-m-SPE.24 Their exceptional
suitability arises from their ultra-high surface areas and
analyte pre-concentration,
porosity,
coupled with unparalleled tunability of pore size and surface
functionality to enhance aﬃnity and selectivity for specic
aatoxins through tailored interactions (e.g., p–p stacking,
hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic eﬀects).25,26 Bimetallic MOFs,
incorporating two distinct metal ions within a single frame-
work, oﬀer
enhanced performance as D-m-SPE
sorbents.27,28 The synergistic interaction between the dual
metals modulates properties such as Lewis acidity, redox
potential, and pore chemistry, leading to improved binding
aﬃnity, selectivity, and chemical stability compared to mono-
metallic analogs.29,30 This design exibility allows for precise
engineering of active sites optimized for strong interaction with
aatoxin molecules, thereby boosting extraction eﬃciency and
matrix tolerance.
